analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 9 finding(s) from 12 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Top-level instantiates dmi_jtag with jtag_hash_i/okey_hash_i/ikey_hash_i and jtag_unlock_o, producing debug_req/debug_req_valid. A debug AXI-lite NOC bridge also drives dm_master AXI signals. dm_master_gnt, dm_master_r_valid, and dm_master_r_rdata are tied off, with comment that system bus accesses are not supported yet. Full debug module request-to-debug_req_o behavior and whether jtag_unlock gates all debug operations were not available in the provided observations.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/trigger debug access / hart debug control and memory/peripheral reachable through dm_master_* / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG/debug interface Operation: read/write/trigger debug access Object: hart debug control and memory/peripheral reachable through dm_master_* Expected guard: debug authorization or jtag_unlock/authentication plus lifecycle/test-mode mediation before debug-originated memory or hart-control access Observed behavior: Top-level instantiates dmi_jtag with jtag_hash_i/okey_hash_i/ikey_hash_i and jtag_unlock_o, producing debug_req/debug_req_valid. A debug AXI-lite NOC bridge also drives dm_master AXI signals. dm_master_gnt, dm_master_r_valid, and dm_master_r_rdata are tied off, with comment that system bus accesses are not supported yet. Full debug module request-to-debug_req_o behavior and whether jtag_unlock gates all debug operations were not available in the provided observations. Path: JTAG/DTM -> dmi_jtag/debug module request/response -> dm_master_* or debug_req_o; separate debug NOC bridge -> dm_master AXI -> dm_slave_* Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If debug requests are accepted without authentication, an external/debug requester could halt harts or access protected memory/peripherals; however the provided evidence does not prove that untrusted JTAG/debug requests reach those controls.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need full debug module instantiation and assignments to debug_req_o/dmactive_o/ndmreset_o plus dmi_jtag internals to prove whether jtag_unlock/authentication gates all debug-originated control.

findings.summary: jtag_unlock assertion resets all register-lock policy bits to zero, allowing lock policy to be cleared outside the normal locked write path.
findings.vulnerability_category: unlock/clear/change lock policy / register-lock control bits for peripheral wrappers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG unlock path and AXI requester Operation: unlock/clear/change lock policy Object: register-lock control bits for peripheral wrappers Expected guard: authenticated JTAG unlock should only enable permitted unlock behavior and should not clear/stall sticky locks after they are set unless explicitly authorized Observed behavior: reglk_ctrl_o is directly composed from reglk_mem. In the sequential write/reset block, reset condition is ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9); therefore jtag_unlock=1 causes all reglk_mem entries to be assigned 'h0. This clears locks rather than preserving sticky lock state. Normal AXI writes are gated by acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl bits, but jtag_unlock bypasses that path by entering the reset branch. Path: reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock -> reglk_mem reset -> reglk_ctrl_o -> wrapper reglk_ctrl_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An asserted JTAG unlock can clear register-lock policy for protected crypto/accounting/peripheral wrappers, potentially re-enabling writes and reads that software lock policy intended to prevent.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need dmi_jtag authentication strength to determine whether jtag_unlock is strictly authenticated, but the reset-on-unlock behavior itself is explicit.

findings.summary: AES0 read mux assigns rdata directly from key0[address] before address-specific read guards, causing key0 words at unhandled key addresses to be readable whenever acct_ctrl_i enables the wrapper.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure/trigger / aes0 key0/key1/key2/key_big/key_big0/state/state_big and control/start registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NOC requester or debug-mode software path Operation: read/write/configure/trigger Object: aes0 key0/key1/key2/key_big/key_big0/state/state_big and control/start registers Expected guard: reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i should gate writes to AES key/control/state registers and reads of secret key/internal state; debug_mode_i should not bypass unless authorized Observed behavior: AXI-lite en is gated with acct_ctrl_i. Writes to start/plaintext and likely keys are guarded by reglk_ctrl_i bits. However the combinational read side initializes rdata to key0[address[8:3]] when en is asserted, before a case statement that only overrides selected public/control/result addresses. For key address indices not handled by the case, the key0 word remains on rdata. debug_mode_i zeros key0/key1 into the core but not the MMIO rdata path observed here. Path: NOC -> aes0_axilite_bridge -> aes0_wrapper address/en/we -> key/state/start registers -> rdata/AES core Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A requester that passes the access-control bit can read AES0 key material from the MMIO read data path despite expected lock-based read suppression, compromising cryptographic secrets.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The truncated write-side segment omitted key write cases, but the observed read-side default assignment is sufficient for a candidate read-exposure finding.

findings.summary: AES1 gates en with acct_ctrl_i. Observed writes to control/config/block/key registers are guarded by reglk_ctrl_i bits. The read-side excerpt shows guarded reads for name/version/control/status/result, but is truncated before any key readback cases or default behavior. debug_mode_i is present as an input, but the provided observations do not show how it affects core_key/rdata.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure/trigger / aes1 key_reg0/key_reg1/key_reg2/core_key/block_reg/core_block/control registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NOC requester or debug-mode software path Operation: read/write/configure/trigger Object: aes1 key_reg0/key_reg1/key_reg2/core_key/block_reg/core_block/control registers Expected guard: reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i should protect writes to key/control registers and prevent reads of key material or stale internal state; debug_mode_i must be authorized if it changes access Observed behavior: AES1 gates en with acct_ctrl_i. Observed writes to control/config/block/key registers are guarded by reglk_ctrl_i bits. The read-side excerpt shows guarded reads for name/version/control/status/result, but is truncated before any key readback cases or default behavior. debug_mode_i is present as an input, but the provided observations do not show how it affects core_key/rdata. Path: NOC -> aes1_axilite_bridge -> aes1_wrapper address/en/we -> key_reg*/block/control -> core1 and rdata Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If key readback or debug behavior is not guarded, AES1 keys or internal state could be exposed or control could be triggered outside policy; provided evidence does not prove such a path.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need complete read-side cases for key_reg*/core_key/block/result and any debug_mode_i handling to decide whether secret readback is blocked.

findings.summary: AES2 gates en with acct_ctrl_i. Observed write side begins with reglk-guarded start and plaintext writes. debug_mode_i masks all three key banks into cii_K0/1/2 before core use. The provided observations did not include the AES2 read mux or full key write cases, so read exposure cannot be confirmed or ruled out.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure/trigger / aes2 key0/key1/key2/cii_K/cii_K0/state/state_big/start/ct / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NOC requester or debug-mode software path Operation: read/write/configure/trigger Object: aes2 key0/key1/key2/cii_K/cii_K0/state/state_big/start/ct Expected guard: reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i should gate AES key/state/control writes and prevent read exposure; debug_mode_i should be mediated by authorization/lock policy Observed behavior: AES2 gates en with acct_ctrl_i. Observed write side begins with reglk-guarded start and plaintext writes. debug_mode_i masks all three key banks into cii_K0/1/2 before core use. The provided observations did not include the AES2 read mux or full key write cases, so read exposure cannot be confirmed or ruled out. Path: NOC -> aes2_axilite_bridge -> aes2_wrapper address/en/we -> key/state/start -> aes2_interface and rdata Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If read/write guards are incomplete, an authorized or misclassified requester could expose AES2 keys/state or trigger encryption outside policy; current evidence is incomplete.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need full AES2 write cases and read-side mux for key/state/ct/status addresses.

findings.summary: HMAC read path exposes inner/outer key-hash bytes through rdata under only reglk_ctrl_i[4], while writes to those secret-derived bytes are protected by a different lock bit reglk_ctrl_i[5].
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure/trigger/expose / HMAC key0, key/hash bytes, message/control registers, internal hash state and outputs / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NOC requester or debug-mode software path Operation: read/write/configure/trigger/expose Object: HMAC key0, key/hash bytes, message/control registers, internal hash state and outputs Expected guard: reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i should protect HMAC key/control writes and prevent reading key/hash intermediate state unless explicitly public; debug_mode_i must not expose/bypass without authorization Observed behavior: HMAC gates en with acct_ctrl_i. key, ikey_hash, and okey_hash data structures are present; debug_mode_i masks key and okey_hash into the core but not ikey_hash. Writes to key0/ikey_hash_bytes/okey_hash_bytes use reglk_ctrl_i[5]. The observed read-side returns ikey_hash_bytes[0] and okey_hash_bytes[7:...] when reglk_ctrl_i[4] is clear, exposing secret-derived hash material via MMIO. The excerpt does not show a debug_mode_i condition in the read path. Path: NOC -> hmac_axilite_bridge -> hmac_wrapper address/en/we -> key/data/hash controls -> hmac core and rdata Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A requester with HMAC access can read key-derived inner/outer hash intermediate values, which may disclose secret material or enable HMAC forgery/analysis depending on how the core uses those values.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full read-side addresses 26-49 are truncated, so direct key0 readback is not fully shown; however ikey/okey hash readback is observed.

findings.summary: acct_wrapper reset default sets all access-control policy words to 0xffffffff, and acc_ctrl_o also ORs one policy word with replicated we_flag, potentially granting access broadly after reset or when we_flag is asserted.
findings.vulnerability_category: write/configure/change access policy / acc_ctrl_o access-control matrix/policy for peripherals / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NOC requester Operation: write/configure/change access policy Object: acc_ctrl_o access-control matrix/policy for peripherals Expected guard: writes to access-control policy registers should require existing privilege/lock guard and must not allow unprivileged requester to grant itself access Observed behavior: Normal acct register writes are gated by acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl bits. However reset initializes every acct_mem entry to 32'hffffffff. acc_ctrl_o is assigned from acct_mem[2], acct_mem[1], and acct_mem[0] OR {8{we_flag}}. Top-level decodes acc_ctrl bits by priv_lvl_i and feeds them directly into wrapper acct_ctrl_i signals. The observed reset default appears permissive, and we_flag can force access bits independently of the stored policy word. Path: NOC -> acct_wrapper -> acc_ctrl_o -> riscv_peripherals acc_ctrl_c -> peripheral acct_ctrl_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: After reset or when we_flag is asserted, access-control policy may grant broad access to protected peripheral wrappers, allowing requesters to configure/read crypto, lock, fuse-related, or policy registers contrary to least privilege.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need semantic definition of acc_ctrl bit polarity to conclusively prove 1 means allow. The naming and use as acct_ctrl_i enable in wrappers strongly suggests asserted bits grant access.

findings.summary: A debug NOC bridge drives dm_master AXI, which is converted to dm_slave_* by axi2mem, but dm_master grant/read-valid/read-data are tied off, indicating system-bus debug master access is not supported in the observed excerpt. DMA NOC ports are present and connected near line 1922, but the destination path and whether those transactions enter the same protected decoder/access-control scheme were not provided.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/route / protected peripheral MMIO regions and memory reachable through DMA/debug master / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMA engine and debug module master Operation: read/write/route Object: protected peripheral MMIO regions and memory reachable through DMA/debug master Expected guard: DMA/debug master transactions should be subject to the same address-range/access-control policy as CPU transactions, including lock/access matrix checks Observed behavior: A debug NOC bridge drives dm_master AXI, which is converted to dm_slave_* by axi2mem, but dm_master grant/read-valid/read-data are tied off, indicating system-bus debug master access is not supported in the observed excerpt. DMA NOC ports are present and connected near line 1922, but the destination path and whether those transactions enter the same protected decoder/access-control scheme were not provided. Path: buf_ariane_dma_noc* and dm_master/debug NOC paths -> protected peripheral or memory regions Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If DMA or debug master transactions can reach protected MMIO without acc_ctrl/reglk checks, they could bypass CPU privilege policy; the provided observations do not establish such reachability.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need DMA bridge body around lines 1900-1970 and any downstream DMA request routing, plus confirmation of whether debug dm_master can reach protected regions despite tied-off responses.

findings.summary: Top-level declares rst_1..rst_11/rst_13 and routes reset inputs into crypto, acct, and reglk wrappers. Observed crypto wrappers clear secret/control/data state on rst_N assertion, which is desirable for secret clearing. acct reset initializes policy to 0xffffffff, and reglk reset clears locks to zero. The provided observations do not include reset-generation logic showing who can assert rst_1..rst_11 or whether reset writes are access-controlled.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset/clear/override / peripheral reset lines and protected state/locks behind them / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software/NOC requester or external reset source Operation: reset/clear/override Object: peripheral reset lines and protected state/locks behind them Expected guard: software-visible or external reset controls for protected peripherals should require privilege/lock/access guard and should clear secret state safely without clearing security locks unexpectedly Observed behavior: Top-level declares rst_1..rst_11/rst_13 and routes reset inputs into crypto, acct, and reglk wrappers. Observed crypto wrappers clear secret/control/data state on rst_N assertion, which is desirable for secret clearing. acct reset initializes policy to 0xffffffff, and reglk reset clears locks to zero. The provided observations do not include reset-generation logic showing who can assert rst_1..rst_11 or whether reset writes are access-controlled. Path: riscv_peripherals reset-control registers/signals rst_1..rst_11/rst_13 -> crypto/reglk/acct wrappers Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If software/NOC can assert reset lines without proper guards, it could clear locks or restore permissive policy while wiping secrets, undermining security policy; evidence is insufficient to attribute reset control to an unprivileged subject.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need reset controller/register decode around top-level rst_1..rst_13 generation and any access-control checks for reset writes.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
